segments.text: Welcome, Mike. We can see you there.
Uh, we have Environment Southland with us as well.
We can see the team there, from the deep south.
Welcome to the Environment Select Committee this morning.
Uh, look, you have got, 15
minutes with us. So look, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your
submission and the main points, and then we'll open up for questions.
And just for those following along today online, we are in two
subcommittees today. So we have Subcommittee A and we have
Subcommittee B. So, if there was a particular,
submission you wanted to hear, just have a look on those different subcommittees.
But look, I'll hand it over to you, to Environment Southland.
Uh, you can hear us all right?
Yes, we can hear you. Thank you.
Uh, that's very good. Uh, look, thank you chair and members of the
committee for the opportunity to present, Southland's perspective on these
important reforms. My name is Jeremy McIhal, chair
of Environment Southland. With me are Phil
Morrison, our deputy chair, and Hayley Fitchett, our
general manager of strategy and reg- regulation.
We're supported by senior staff, who can assist with technical matters
if required. Environment Southland is responsible for
implementing national direction on, on the ground, across
freshwater, land use, natural hazards, and flood
protection, working in enduring partnership with mana
whenua and our communities. With that
context, I'll hand to our deputy chair, Phil Morrison, to outline the key
issues we wish to highlight today.
Uh, thank you. So we support the intent of these
reforms. The current system is too slow, too complex, and
not delivering the outcomes New Zealand needs.
But our central message is this: if these bills are not
adjusted in a small number of critical areas, they risk
replacing one set of problems with another set, more
complex, less coherent, and harder to make workable
in practice. You have our full written submission.
Today, we will focus on four issues we believe are
decisive to whether this s- system
succeeds or fails. Issue one, system
fragmentation. The split between the planning
bill and the natural environment bill creates a structural
risk. On paper, the system is designed as a
coherent funnel with aligned goals, national direction,
and integrated plans. However, there is no single clear
purpose across both bills to guide how these
systems should operate together.
In practice, councils will be required to operate two
legislative systems at once, each with potentially
different purposes, tools, and decision pathways.
If integration is not explicitly required and
enforced, fragmentation will occur at three
levels: at strategic planning level, at
plan-making level, and most critically, at the consenting
interface. And when that happens, the burden does not sit
with central government. It sits with councils and communities
trying to reconcile conflicting signals.
That will introduce delay, cost, and litigation,
exactly what this reform is trying to remove.
So our position is simple. If two acts proceed,
integration must be a binding system principle,
not an assumption.
Issue two, centralization and regional workability.
We support strong national direction.
Clear environmental limits and consistent standards are
necessary. We also strongly support regional spatial
planning. But the f- the bills go further
than direction. They shift
control. National instruments will determine
most key settings, leaving limited space for regional
judgment. The risk is not
theoretical. We have seen in the past that highly prescribed
national frameworks can be difficult to
implement, expensive to administer, and
poorly adapted to regional conditions.
In Southland, this matters.
We are a resource-dependent region, where economy,
environment, and infrastructure are tightly coupled with
specific challenges such as wetland loss and
cumulative land use effects. We have demonstrated
that regional collaboration works, for example, through our intensive winter
grazing work with stakeholders. This led to changes that made
regulation more workable while still achieving environmental
outcomes. So if regional flexibility is too
constrained, councils will be held accountable for outcomes
that they no longer have the tools to influence.
That represents a design failure, not an
implementation failure.
Issue three, environmental limits and
pathways. We strongly support limits.
They are essential. But limits alone do not improve environmental
outcomes. Limits require pathways,
data, staging, tools, and time to
transition. Many critical elements, such as
methodologies and management units, are yet to be
defined, and in areas like freshwater, limits are not
yet supported by complete or consistent
data. Without flexibility in how limits are applied and
phased, we risk creating a system that is technically
sound but lacks workability in
practice.This creates a risk that limits are either
set too conservatively, delaying outcomes, or too
ambitiously, which makes them unworkable.
And neither of those outcomes builds confidence.
We also note that prioritising caps ahead of broader
tools such as catchment act- action plans
risks undermining the very approaches that have proven effective in
regions like ours. So we need a system that enables
progress towards limits, not just compliance
with limits. Issue four,
regulatory relief. While we recognise the intent
of regulatory relief, the proposal raises serious
equity and practical concerns. It introduces
a mechanism that effectively compensates for
environmental protections on a property by property
basis, and this risks three outcomes.
First, it undermines spatial planning by potentially
fragmenting decisions. Second, the risk of creating
inequity between landowners. And third, placing significant
and uncertain financial liability on councils and rate
payers.
For regions like Southland with a small rating base, that exposure
is significant.
In effect, the system begins to treat environmental protection as a
cost to be offset rather than a foundation to be
maintained. And that is a fundamental shift in the
logic of the system, and it risks weakening public
trust in that system. So we recommend that these
provisions be reconsidered and that alternative mechanisms,
potentially outside the core system, be explored to
incentivise positive environmental actions such as
biodiversity and wetland restoration.
To round out the points made by Councillor Morrison, a one size fits
all approach risks increasing rather than decreasing regulatory
workload. We would caution the committee about accepting the
idea that the bills, as drafted, will significantly reduce
regulatory workload for both the regulated and the
regulator. Part of the bills, including the registration of
permitted activities and the accompanying workload, will shift effort
and expense rather than remove it.
In summary, this reform is ambitious and
necessary, but its success will depend less on its intent
and more on whether the system is coherent, integrated and
workable in practice. We've highlighted four issues
today: system in- integration, appropriate
centralization, workable environmental limits, and regulatory
relief. These are not peripheral issues, they are system
defining choices. If addressed well, this reform can
succeed. If not, we risk embedding a new generation of
complexity into the system, one that is harder to implement
and harder for communities to trust.
Thank you, and we welcome your questions.
Look, thank you very much for that submission.
Uh, very clear, and a, a really well written submission, so we
appreciate your time on that. Uh, look, we've got a couple of questions.
Uh, we'll move to Priyanka first.
Thank you. Um, and thanks for your submission today.
Um, I had a quick question around environmental limits and the point
that you've made in your submit- written submission, that you
support regional councils a- and, you know, feel that they're best
placed to set, environmental limits and
implement them. We've had other submitters make the point
around, nationwide consistency and the
fact that this may not lead to nationwide
consistency, and some asking for some sort
of a national framework within which regional councils have some
flexibility to make changes, that are more
suited to their area. What would your thoughts be on something like
that?
Thank you for the question. I'll direct this question to Karen Wilson,
who's our General Manager of Science.
Um, so I would, concur with the idea
that, I guess, nationally consistent frameworks are not going to
lead to nationally consistent outcomes.
Um, the environments that they get applied to, can
be very different, across the country.
So in order to sort of achieve a similar level of outcome,
there needs to be taken into account the localized,
the localized system. So yeah, I would concur with the other
submissions, on that one.
So you would agree that some sort of a nationwide framework
within which you can make some changes would be better than just regional
councils setting limits?
Agreed.
Okay. Thank you.
And David McLeod.
Yes. And thank you, also for the submission that you've provided.
Um, you mentioned about regulatory relief.
It's a, it's a very common, submission that's been brought before us
itself. Um, and of course it's based on the premise of,
any new, imposition placed on a, on a landowner,
is not left out of pocket. They don't go backwards negatively, a, a major
imposition. Um, what, what in your mind are some of the solutions?
You didn't quite get to the solutions.
You, you spoke about that you don't believe it should be in, within this.
Could you t- talk, talk about that a bit more?
Uh, thank you for your question. Um, we spoke in our submission, about
potentially looking at a positively framed system
rather... So, in terms of incentivization-
Uh, for improvements to particular aspects such
as biodiversity, the enhancement of wetlands.
Uh, wetland loss, as we've identified in our submission as a, as a critical
concern and matter within Southland.
We are leaning in very heavily into non-regulatory methods.
But the addition of positively framed
incentivization tools within a regulatory framework we
feel could be, a substantial improvement to the
system to further double down on the good work that
our communities, our farming communities, our runanga
are already making in the restoration enhancement,
of, of wetlands, for example. Similarly with other forms
of biodiversity in Southland.
Thank you.
Thank you. And one last question from Steve Abel.
Thank you, Madam Chair. I just wanted you to expand on your comments regarding
regulatory relief. You say that it's, not
workable, that it seems to set councils up to
fail, and risks undermining the natural environment.
I wonder if you can expand on some of those comments, please.
Certainly. Some of my
colleagues
Oh, we seem to have lost your audio there.
Um.
Uh, we still can't hear you.
No.
Oh, okay. Um.
Um, we can't hear you.
No? Okay. Um.
Oh, there we go. If you can move a bit closer, maybe we might be able to hear you
to the speaker.
Okay. Just one moment. Move a bit closer.
There we go. We can hear you, yeah.
Yes. Yeah, okay. Otherwise I was hoping that Mike would jump in online.
Um, so the questions are on the workability of regulatory
relief. The challenge in Southland is that we have such a small
ratings base that covers a substantial,
area, and
we would have to make extraordinarily difficult trade-off
choices, because of the cost, of
potentially for regulatory relief to us, either through direct
financial cost or the opportunity cost of, for example, if we would use
ratings relief. So, you know, co- re- revenue
forgone. Um, and so we would be concerned
that we wouldn't then be able to fulfill our statutory
obligations with respect of things like the protection of
biodiversity, for example. Um, and it would be, in a
governance sense, extraordinarily difficult.
Is there anything that my colleagues would like to add on that?
I think central from all of...
The only, only thought I would add is that if, that once you introduce
regulatory relief, there's an asymmetry in the system in that there's no provision
for environmental relief, and at some point in the future
there is potential for, government to
potentially consider rebalancing that asymmetry by introducing the concept
of environmental relief as well. And I think longer term, that's something that we
need to take account of and consider, the potential for that kind
of subsequent intervention.
Look, thank you. Uh, we have met time, but we
have many questions, but we do appreciate, a very thorough
and insightful submission, you know, covering the, the many areas there
for consideration. So thank you to you and your team
today.
Uh, look, thanks, thanks for the opportunity to, to speak to you.
And, and just in closing, I would just like to say, look, highly
important this work, but it needs to
take time to get it right. Make sure we get it right, and,
if it takes a bit longer, the timelines don't matter
here. It's a once in generation change, so that's all I'd like to say.
So thanks again for the opportunity.
Look, no, we appreciate that, and we are very aware of the significance of, of this
reform, so thank you.
Thank you.
So we now move to our next submitter, New Zealand Notable
Trees Trust, who I can see there. Uh,
Brad, welcome to the select committee this morning.
Look, you have 15 minutes with us, so we will pass it over to you to
talk us through your submission, and then we'll open up for
questions.
Oh, thank you, Madam Chair and members of the committee for the,
opportunity to, appear today.
My name is Brad Kadvalider, and I'm speaking on behalf of the New Zealand
Notable Trees Trust. The trust works
nationally to identify and record and support the
protection of Aotearoa, New Zealand's historic and
notable trees, working with landowners,
territorial authorities, and
community. Our submission focuses
specifically on how the proposed resource management
reforms may affect the identification and protection of
historic and notable trees.
Trees in general provide a broad range of well-documented
benefits. They improve air quality, moderate
urban heat, support biodiversity,
and contribute to the physical and wellbeing of our
communities. However, historic and
notable trees occupy a very special category
within the wider urban forest. These are often
mature or veteran specimens with exception...
exceptional age, size, rarity,
cultural association, or landscape
prominence. Some are early plantings
linked to the history of settlement.
Others are remarkable indigenous specimens representing
ecological commu- continuity.
Unlike buildings and infrastructure, a mature tree
cannot simply be replaced.
When a historic or notable tree is lost-
The accumulated ecological landscape and heritage
values developed over decades or
centuries are permanently lost.
These trees therefore function as a long-term
environmental and cultural infrastructure within
our communities.
It is also worth noting that New Zealand contains many tree
specimens that are exceptional by international
standards. Our cool temperate climate has allowed
a, a number of early exotic plantings to reach dimensions rarely
seen within their natural ranges.
At the same time, mature indigenous trees attract significant
international attention because New Zealand's native flora is
globally distinctive. Together,
these trees form what is effectively a living environmental
and cultural collection across the
country. Their protection is therefore not simply a
local amenity issue, it's part of the long-term
stewardship of nationally si- significant
natural assets.
Protection of notable trees has been part of New Zealand's planning system for
decades. Most scheduled trees have been
identified and protected with landowner
knowledge, and in many cases were nominated by
landowners themselves, often as a legacy for future
generations.
Properties containing protected trees
are commonly purchased in full knowledge of those
protections.
This means protection is not generally an unexpected
regulatory imposition,
it is an established part of the planning framework attached
to those properties.
Ultimately, the trust's concerns is not whether
reform occurs, but how it is
implemented. There are three areas of concern
the trust wish to highlight.
Firstly, the regulatory relief provisions.
If notable tree scheduling were to trigger regulatory
relief mechanisms, councils may face
fiscal and legal exposure. Over time,
that
risk could discourage councils from retaining or introducing
notable tree protections.
Secondly, institutional responsibility.
The current Resource Management Act clearly empowers territorial
authorities to identify and protect
historic and notable trees through district
plans.
The proposed legislation introduces uncertainty about
whether this function remains clearly embedded in the planning
system.
And our third concern, that there is a potential
mismatch with heritage
frameworks. Many notable trees are
protected not only for historic heritage
reasons, but also for botanical,
ecological, landscape and amenity values.
If protection were to rely primarily on heritage
legislation, there is a risk that many currently
protected trees would fall outside that
framework.
There is also a practical governance issue.
New Zealand currently does not have a consolidated national
inventory of legally protected historic and
notable trees. Protection
currently occurs through district plan schedules maintained
independently
by territorial authorities. Without a
verified national baseline identifying
how many trees are protected, where they are protected, and
the values for which they are recognized, it is difficult to assess
the scale of any function transfer or
reform.
Structural reform of this nature should proceed on the basis of a
clearly established evidential foundation.
The trust therefore recommends three key actions.
Firstly, the legislation should explicitly
retain statutory provisions, enabling territorial
authorities to identify and schedule notable
trees, and ensure that regulatory relief mechanisms
do not discourage councils from retaining or
introducing those protections.
Secondly, existing district plan schedules
should transition into the new framework without loss
of protection.
And lastly, if responsibility for notable tree
protection is to be altered or centralized, this
should occur only after a nationally coordinated
audit establishes a clear baseline of the
protected estate.
In closing, historic and notable trees are
long-lived public assets. Their protection is
proportionate, widely understood, and embedded in New
Zealand's planning practice. The trust's
central point is simply that reform should strengthen, not
inadvertently weaken, those long-standing protections.
With appropriate legislative
clarification, these reforms present an opportunity to
strengthen consistency, transparency, and
long-term stewardship of the historic and notable trees in our
communitiesThank you very much for the opportunity to,
present today. I'd be happy to answer any questions from the
committee. Thank you.
Well, thank you, Brad. And, and thank you for your submission,
because obviously it's a very, interesting area-
Thank you
... that we haven't traversed actually, fully yet.
So look, Steve has got a question, so we'll go
first to Steve Abel.
Yeah. Thank you so much. A, a really excellent submission,
and you made it very clear. I, I wonder
would it be correct to reflect that, the great
value that those notable trees offer
the community, I think particularly in urban environments,
is an excellent example of a commons,
if you like. Um, a thing that has
broad community value for its provision of beauty,
shade, ecology, history, and
therefore something that, that
benefits us all
and can't be captured by the simple idea
of, the private property rights of the person on
whose property the tree stands when that tree has
outlived them and will outlive them.
Um,
so this is a sort of an excellent example of why we need to have
a protect- a special status for these nationally significant
trees.
Yes, absolutely. The, the, the, the values of, of
these trees extend well beyond, the, the property
boundaries. Uh, the community absolutely shares, in
the stories of those trees and, they,
they're, often... You know,
when these trees come under threat, communities rise up, so
it shows really the, the significance the co- community
hold in the, these individual trees.
Can I do a quick sup on it, Madam Chair?
We, we... Yeah, a quick sup.
It's very quick. Uh, you've talked about a consolidated national inve-
inventory.
Yes.
Do you think that's a thing we should do anyway, that that would be hugely
beneficial? Because I realize that these schedules are held in disparate
spaces.
Yes, yes. We, we've tried to, you know, get councils
to bring, you know, bring them together but, you know, they all basically
work in- independently. Uh, trying to,
search across the various district plans is, is quite a, an
onerous task. Some of the listings
include avenues, so they might include,
90 trees in a, a single line listing-
True
... whereas another line listing would be one, a
tree.
Yes.
And the other, the other aspect is that,
some of these trees have, are listed for
historic purposes and others for their general
amenity.
So it's quite a... Yeah.
Thank you. Well, now over to Kevin Maxwell.
I think, I think that would be it. Thank you, Chair.
Uh, Brad, I... In
your comment you're saying, you know, it goes, the value of these trees goes well
beyond, the private property, and as a community shares in the
value. I'm aware of,
trees being cut down before they hit the height
in a place called Papamoa of, what would become automatically
listed as a, a notable tree. Now,
would you think that if a property owner who, had, say, a
Norfolk Pine could see themselves being,
compensated for what you've called the community sharing in the value through
regulatory relief might incentivize people to see, these
trees, as value to them as well, be
recognized by the community, not just the cost being
held by the property owner?
Well, firstly, I'm not really aware that, that, there
is a local authority that automatically protects the tree
Uh, this is in, in the Tauranga City Council, this is what used to happen.
It doesn't happen anymore, I grant you.
Right. Yes.
But it used to happen.
Yeah. Yeah. That's right.
It's-
That, that gen- general prote- tree protection is, is no longer,
possible for local authorities, but, um-
But this is my point. If it was to be, uh...
If there was to be a compensation, people might see the value in, in allowing these
trees to become, recognized on a list that the
community shares.
Uh, yes, I guess that would be correct.
Uh, how other local authorities compensate,
landowners as they share in the maintenance and inspection of those trees.
So there are, you know, many ways councils can
incentivize.
Look, thank you very much, Brad, for your submission today.
Uh, we have met time. Uh, we appreciate your time,
and that perspective, obviously on
protecting our trees. Uh, very important submission, so thank you
very much for that. We appreciate it.
Thank you very much for the time.
Bye.
So now we move to our next submitter, the
Hastings District Council.
And I can see a couple of familiar faces there- ...
from Hastings. Welcome. Welcome to the select
committee this morning. Uh, look, you've got 15 minutes with us,
so, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission,
and then we'll open up for questions.
So over to you.
Uh, morning, and thank you for allowing us to add to what I understand is in
excess of four hundred hours of submissions.
Um, so we will try and keep this brief, and we, we look forward to receiving your
questions at the end. Um, so I'm Wendy Shullam of the Hastings
District Council, and to my right is Caleb Sutton, who is our
environmental consents manager here at HDC.
Um, thank you first of all for the opportunity to speak today, and
as you likely have gathered, I am appearing on behalf of the Hastings District
Council. Um, our council is, very
supportive in principle, and especially with the efficient and
cost-effective planning and resource management system and the
government's resource management reform, package
objectives. They align really well with what we believe as well,
particularly with relation to the regional spatial
plans. Um, so while we are broadly supportive, our
submission today is going to focus on a few key concerns with the proposed
bills. Um, and these, these topics will cover things like
climate change, regional spatial planning, transitional
arrangements, and the practicality of the new system for councils
and communities. So what I'd like to do today is just
briefly connect those concerns to what that actually means for
us on the ground in terms of communities like
Hastings. Uh, at present, we understand that
approximately sixty-one percent of New Zealand is in some form of
recovery, and while we are three years on from Cyclone Gabrielle
here in Heretaunga, the impact is still significant, and it
still scars our lands and people. Um, and it's for that
reason that I really want to begin today with a mention of the absence of
any explicit reference to climate change within the goals of the
bills. Um, this is a significant concern for
Hastings, as many of our recent decisions have relied
heavily on climate-related considerations.
In our view, climate change is not a peripheral issue for
planning, it's central. Uh, climate change underpi-
underpins for us coastal hazards across the Hawke's Bay
coastline, and it is increasingly determining where
and how we develop safely. In practical terms,
it's about whether we are enabling homes, infrastructure, and
investment in places that will still be safe and viable
in thirty to fifty years' time. Without climate change
explicitly recognized as a goal, we do not believe that the
regional spatial plans can provide the strategic direction the
legislation intends.
And without national direction, we risk fragmented and inconsistent
responses, repeating the piecemeal outcomes under previous
legislation, and that's something we would like to help avoid.
Uh, that means that we could end up in situations where different regions make
different calls about risk, and in some cases, that could mean
continuing allow- allowing development in areas that will
ultimately put people and property in harm's
way. So while we ins- support the intent of the
bills, we are also concerned about the lack of an overarching
statement clearly tying the two together.
For Hastings, this is particularly important when balancing our
highly productive land with growth and property
rights. They are two tensions that we navigate carefully here in
Heretaunga. Our urban areas are
surrounded by Class One and Two soils, and protecting
them is central to our economy. These soils are not
just land, they are the foundation of our
horticulture, sector, our jobs, and our
region's prosperity. Without clear integrated
purpose, these matters may be treated
inconsistently. And on the ground, that creates uncertainty
for landowners, investors, and our councils, and it
increases the likelihood of conflict, delays, and costly
legislation.
Litigation.
Uh, oh, sorry, litigation. Apologies.
Um,
with regards to the regional spatial plans, we are concerned about the
key national direction, and how it may not be available in
time to inform the first generation of regional spatial
plans. Uh, to ensure those plans achieve the intended
outcomes, we suggest, extending the statutory
time frames until the national direction is in place.
Uh, preparing these plans are co- are costly and complex
exercises, significantly more than the future development
strategies, a process we've just been through.
And in our experience, we suggest that it will require at least two years to
complete. If we are required to move too quickly
without clear direction, we risk doing the work twice,
once to meet deadlines, and again to fix it later.
And in our view, that's not good use of ratepayer funding.
Sufficient time is also needed for meaningful community, iwi, and
hapū participation. If we get this stage wrong,
we may lock in poor planning decisions for
decades. We're also concerned about
the proposed regulatory, relief regime, which
I know I've heard other submitters, making points about as well, but I
will reiterate from our perspective.
Uh, we believe that mandated or nationally directed provisions should
not trigger relief. Provisions already settled under the
RMA should be excluded,
and there is a real risk of increased, litigation,
under the current framework. In simple terms, this
could inadvertently create a system where councils are required to
compensate for rules that are legally required-- we are legally required
to implement. That is a fundamental contradiction in our
view. Instead of reducing cost and
complexity, this mechanism risks becoming a forum for disputes
over eligibility, scope, and valuation.That
means more time, more cost, and more pressure on rate
payers, not less. It also diverts funding away from
core responsibilities like infrastructure and natural hazard
resilience, which are already under significant pre- pressure,
particularly here. We recommend that the regulatory relief
framework be removed entirely from both f- bills.
However, if it is to be retained, we do believe it must be
tightened, and tightly limited to genuinely
new or bespoke provisions, and must exclude mandated or
carried over RMA rules.
We support the nationally standardized provisions for efficiency.
However, rigid, rigid standardization rules or, or
risks weakening local protections that are well, working well
already. For example, in Hastings, our plains production
zone has successfully protected our highly productive land and
managed lifestyle sub, subdivision.
That has been critical to preventing the gradual loss of our best
soils through fragmented development.
A standardized rural production zone may not
achieve the same outcome. That is a significant risk for our
community. If that protection is weakened, the
change is effectively irreversible.
We cannot grow on land we have paved over.
Once highly productive land is subdivided, we cannot restore
it. We support a pathway for bespoke
provisions at a local level. We justify...
We, we are justified, I need to emphasize that.
With decisions undertaken at a local governance level rather than by the
minister to avoid unnecessary delay and
over-centralization. We are also
concerned that the bill does not require mandatory accre-
accreditation for hearings commissioners, and I speak for, from that view
also as previously accredita- accredited commissioner.
All RMA commissioners currently must hold the Making Good Decisions
Accreditation, which ensures baseline competency in
law, natural justice, tikanga, and hearing
procedures. Without that safeguard, there is a real risk of
inconsistent decision-making, which can undermine confidence
and increase appeals. We recommend requiring mandatory
accreditation or equivalent competency standards for all
commissioners.
We recommend a retail, we recommend retail distribution
effects are included in the scope of the planning bill.
This is particularly important to our community.
This is fundamentally how we shape our towns and
cities. Managing retail distribution supports compact
settlement, housing dens- density around centers, and
strong commercial hubs. Without it, development can become
scattered, weakening our town centers, increasing
infrastructure costs, and putting more pres- pressure on our valuable
productive land. For Hastings, this directly rela-
links to protecting our fertile growing soils and maintaining our strong
local economy.
So in closing, Hastings District Council does support the intent of
the reform, but urges the panel to strengthen the bills
by explicitly embedding climate change
adaptation, providing clear integration between the two
bills, allowing realistic timeframes for
regional spatial plans, ensuring meaningful Māori
participation, avoiding mechanisms that
increase legislation and uncertainty.
These changes are not about slowing reform.
They're about making sure that the system works in practice, not
just in principle. Um, thank you for the opportunity to
present, and we're very happy to take any questions.
Look, thank you very much, Wendy, and thank you for that
submission. Um, obviously a lot of experience,
in terms of, climate adaptation and, and mitigation, having been
through Cyclone Gabrielle. So look, I might just take the, the privilege of
asking the first question, given Hastings is very familiar to
me. Um,
when, when you talk about climate adaptation and mitigation being in the goals,

We-we're gonna have another look at that project," or, "I'm gonna have another look
to that project, that I
abandoned." The second point I'd like to make
is to urge, members of Parliament to work together to
forge a non-partisan consensus on RMA reform and other housing
policy. Um, and you know, I note that the work of
the previous labor done by the Honorable Phil Twyford to bring about
changes to the planning system to increase housing supply, and we applaud
that. Um, and we also applaud the fact the current government has taken this
work and run with it and, and taken it further.
So it's really positive.
One of the most damaging things for the sector is, it's a sector that
invests in expensive long run infrastructure, you could almost call
it.Um, one of the worst things for it is, is flip-flopping government
policy as governments change. So we need long-term planning and
consensus on the key issues. Um, it looks to us from the outside that there
is a level of agreement there, and I hope this is, this is the case in
reality. Um, and my third and final point is to make sure that
the overarching goals of this reform aren't undermined by the detail and
implementation. It's important that the intent of this bill is
delivered, and, it, while it's important that communities have
control over how our towns and cities grow,
there's gotta be a balance between that and providing sufficient
housing, for areas of growing demand and, and of course, for our young
people. Um, and I, I'm
talking here when I, I think about this, things like development contributions for
example, which on the one hand, good, 'cause they provide essential
funding, but on the other can be used as a lever to sort of constrain and
control, development that takes place, can
act as a brake on the ability to new-- build new houses and add
considerably to the cost. So that's one area we'd love to see central and local
government work together to deliver a funding mechanism for the necessary
infrastructure, but that's fairer than just putting the cost
or, or most of that cost or a large chunk of it on the first person to buy a new
house. Um, in our submission we talked about other areas where
perhaps the law could be tightened up and as I said again, we're not lawyers,
but these were just suggestions, you know, such as binding statutory
timeframes with consequences, if they're missed, and
po- potentially naturally-- nationally standardized provisions as
the default with a high bar for bespoke local
departures from those provisions.
Um, there may be other areas which could be tightened up to make consenting faster
and less costly, while maintaining
standards. Uh, we just ask you to keep this in mind as you progress the
bill. Um, that's all from me, my speaking.
I'm happy to take questions, and I commend you all for your
work.
Look Matt, thank you very much, for that very helpful
submission. Um, lots to consider there.
Look, we'll go to a question, and we'll go to Cameron Luxton
first.
Thank you Chair. Um, thank you Matt for this submission.
I mean, you know, there's been reports come out recently about the huge amount of
benefit that, the, the property investors, of New
Zealand provide, and it sounds like what you're saying and, and I think
we heard explicitly that restricted development lifting prices isn't helping
anybody, let alone property investors, and actually having a functioning
planning system is what's needed.
Yeah.
But I'm interested in these obstacles that you've talked about.
Could you, could you explain in your, you said in your travels you've
talked to, people who want to do developments but have been held back, what
obstacles are there? And one of the ones you specifically
mentioned was, development contributions being a high
cost. And so my sup-- on, on the original question about
obstacles, and I'll ask it now is, do you see any benefit from
the amendments that are being put through on the infrastructure funding and
financing bill or act? Sorry.
Um, I'll, I'll address that one first.
I, I don't know the detail of that bill.
I have to admit I haven't read that in detail, but I absolutely agree with the
intent and thrust of that. Um, definitely
finding different ways to, provide funding for the infrastructure is a real
positive, and we applaud that approach.
Um, and there have been other suggestions.
I think at the last election there were some policy ch- suggestions around shar-
sharing GST, for new housing. Um, that's not
a bad suggestion either. I'm sure there are other ways of doing
this. In terms of specific things,
sometimes it can just be very nitpicky things at the
local council level, and I won't name specific councils
here, but some of the sort of activities are an
investor, of ours who demolished some old
really life expired sort of properties and wanted to build new
properties, single bedroom apartments,
to replace what was there. And they were told by the council that they
couldn't do that. They couldn't replace, what
was there with s- one room apartments, and these were
the apartments that actually met the demand.
The developer, the, the, our member had actually done the research.
They had to replace what was there
exactly. So they had to build a two-bedroom apartment as well
as another couple of one bedrooms.
There were various other elements of that particular development that
added cost. Um, some of it was around
trees, some planting. Um, but that's
just one example. Um, I have another member, this is one who
has said that they are now looking at restarting a project.
Um, and again, it was rules
around, um... I'm trying to remember this correctly.
It was, it was to do with the proximity to a footpath, I think, and also certain
rules around, verandas that had to be put on a,
a property, in this area. Again, they
could have knocked down an old weather board house and replaced it with
new, you know, modern, double glazed, all of that sort
of stuff, and that's a development that's just got stopped in its
tracks. So I get lots of these stories around the country.
Can't remember them all.
Yeah.
Um, but they're there. So anything that can be done
to, to streamline that and to remove those obstacles would be fantastic.
Okay. So if I may supplementary to that-
Yes
... Chair. So, so would, would the Property Investors Federation
be in support of effects outside, the scope of this
act, being persons exercising performing functions can have regard
to the internal or external layout of buildings, and
having that put in this new act will see more homes being built that
New Zealanders need?
Yeah, we would support that. We want more homes to be built that New Zealanders
need. We're not scared of this. Um, you know, we don't rely on a
shortage of houses to, to run a
business. What we rely on is having a good solid
supply of houses and operating our businesses efficiently,
and, and attractively to our customers.
Thank you.
Um, Matt, I might just jump in and ask around regulatory relief
because it's come up, many times on this committee through the
process. I'd be interested to get the perspective, I suppose, from the
property investors, and I suppose what you're finding,
you know, when you're obviously, from a property
owner's perspective.
Uh, yes, that's something we absolutely supported, the idea of
regulatory relief where, they pr-
where the, the,
controls impose significant burden, I think is the wording that's
used. Um,
yes, that is actually really important.
There are a lot of people who put in a lot of money, to get
projects up and running, and sometimes unnecessarily, and we would
like to see something that really
focuses the mind of the planning authorities
on what they are doing and the costs that they are imposing.
So that is one of the things we
absolutely support.
Yeah. Okay. Look, thank you very much Matt.
It appears we have no further questions, but, look, a really interesting
submission. Um, appreciate your time on it and, coming before the
committee today. So, thank you very much.
Thank you very much. Appreciate the time.
So members, that draws us to the end of our morning
session. So we will now, break for lunch and resume
at twelve
thirty.
